topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure lay spread across the worn coffee table, the architectural renderings of the gleaming new complex mocking the faded wallpaper of my current unit. I traced the outline of the balcony in the picture, a slow, deliberate movement that felt brittle under my fingertips. Every time I glanced toward the window, the view of the familiar, slightly chipped brick wall seemed to shrink, replaced by the imagined infinity pool and minimalist furniture. I started spending hours rearranging the few remaining decorative items, a frantic attempt to inject some semblance of vibrancy into a space that was rapidly becoming obsolete. When the management finally announced the vote, the silence in the hallway felt thick, suffocating. I kept my expression neutral, forcing a small, tight smile when Mrs. Henderson commented on the impending change, but inside, a sharp, unpleasant knot tightened in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 2]

"Are you going to look at this?" Mark asked, holding up the glossy pamphlet. I didn't look up from the stack of old mail I was sorting, my jaw clenching almost imperceptibly. The news—the entire building converting to luxury condos—had landed like a physical blow. I felt a sudden, hot flush creep up my neck, and I instinctively pulled my cardigan tighter around myself, as if trying to physically shield myself from the bright future being thrust upon everyone else. I started tapping my foot rapidly against the floorboards, a restless energy buzzing beneath my skin. Later, when I walked past the elevator, I kept my gaze fixed on the polished brass, focusing intently on the reflection, unwilling to meet anyone's eyes.

[story 3]

I spent the afternoon meticulously cleaning every surface, scrubbing the grout lines until they shone, a pointless exercise in trying to salvage something tangible from the impending demolition. The reality of the condo conversion settled over me like a heavy, damp blanket. Every time a neighbor cheerfully discussed their plans for a spacious, open-concept living area, a sour taste rose in my mouth. I found myself staring at the door to my own apartment, imagining it being stripped bare, its familiar imperfections erased by pristine white walls and expensive fixtures. When the official notice arrived, I didn't cry or shout. Instead, I methodically filed the papers, my movements stiff and overly precise, a quiet fury simmering just beneath the surface of my composure.
